assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following is INCORRECT about the *Augustus of Primaporta*?**

![Augustus of Primaporta, 1st century C.E., marble, 2.03 m high (Vatican Museums)](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/8b2bf0f6730893cae511a0669e825a2b.jpeg)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: This sculpture of the Roman emperor Augustus draws on the ancient Greek tradition of idealized figures. Sculpture from the Republic period of patricians emphasized age and realism.

choices.content: This statue is modeled on Patrician sculpture from the Roman republic.
This statue was found in the Villa of Livia; Livia was Augustus's wife.
This statue shows Augustus as a  brilliant military leader.
This statue shows Augustus as having divine ancestry.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is the only large-scale bronze sculpture of an emperor on his horse to survive from antiquity.**


![Equestrian Sculpture of Marcus Aurelius, c. 173–176 C.E. (Capitoline Museums, Rome)](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/12cdc0a3194bdfaa6d3dfabdeb081f31.jpeg)
*Equestrian Sculpture of Marcus Aurelius*, c. 173-76 C.E. (Capitoline Museums, Rome)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Many bronze sculptures were melted down at some point in history and the bronze was used to make other things.

assessment_items.question_content:
**The artist of the *Ludovisi Battle Sarcophagus* followed earlier Roman models and based this active and chaotic composition on earlier Roman work.**


![Battle of the Romans and Barbarians (Ludovisi Battle Sarcophagus), c. 250–260 C.E. (Museo Nazionale Romano-Palazzo Altemps, Rome)](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/2673c1df0b295d8ea6da05d1ed03d10d.jpeg)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The *Ludovisi Battle Sarcophagus* reflects a change in artistic style that coincides with growing instability in the Roman Empire.

assessment_items.question_content:
The Ara Pacis commemorated Augustus' military victory at the Battle of Actium.


![Ara Pacis Augustae (Altar of Augustan Peace), 9 B.C.E. (Ara Pacis Museum, Rome, Italy)](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ea314a859fe1e38944b10feb13b4d100.jpeg)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The Ara Pacis ("Altar of Peace") commemorates the great peace that was achieved by Augustus (the Pax Romana).

assessment_items.question_content:
**The ground level columns of the Colosseum use which of the classical orders?**

![Unadorned column, Colosseum (Flavian Amphitheater, or Amphitheatrum Flavium), c. 70–80 C.E., Rome](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/9c39a5f5c9edf17edd5b18b45a5f638b.jpeg)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The Tuscan order is very simple and is similar to the Doric order, but the columns have bases and are not fluted.

assessment_items.question_content:
The Colosseum and the Pantheon share a structural material for which Roman engineers became famous. **What is that material?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: This human-made material allowed Romans to quickly build large open spaces with less skilled workers.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following is true of ancient Roman sculpture (choose all that apply)?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Ancient Roman sculpture was often painted, and strongly influenced by ancient Greek art. In fact, much of what we know from ancient Greek art comes from later Roman copies.
Ancient Roman sculpture like the frieze on the  Ara Pacis and the sculpture of Augustus of Primaporta, clearly reflect political ideology.

During the earlier period of the Roman Republic, their sculpture often demonstrated a concern for realism. Later, during the period of the empire, ideal forms borrowed from the Greeks were favored. 

Watch [this video](/humanities/art-history/art-history-400-c-e--ancient-cultures-1/ancient-rome/v/veristic-male-portrait) to review.

choices.content: Ancient Roman sculpture was influenced by ancient Greek art.
Ancient Roman sculpture was often painted.
Ancient Roman sculpture often reflected political ideologies.
Ancient Roman sculpture sometimes reflected the Greek ideals of youth and beauty and sometimes the more Roman Republican ideals of old age and wisdom.

assessment_items.question_content: Here is a doric, ionic and corinthian capital. Put these  in the chronological order of their development. [[☃ orderer 1]]
hints: The doric is the oldest and simplest order, the Corinthian is the latest and most complex.

correct_options: ![Plain, unadorned column from "Hera II," temple likely dedicated to Hera](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/a222904b65027e40cfad5fe05209f14a.jpeg)
![Column with volutes (scroll-like ornaments) on the capital, North Porch of the Erechtheion](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/45fbf88858f114bd0857050b78f304aa.jpeg)
![Column with elaborate vegetal ornaments, including stylized, carved leaves of an acanthus plant, on the capital, The Pantheon](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/8d23f4a1851b2c6806b63bd8396d463a.jpeg)

assessment_items.question_content:
Match these monuments with the Emperor who commissioned them.
[[☃ categorizer 1]]
hints: The *Ara Pacis* was commissioned by Augustus in commemoration of the peace he established in the empire.
Vespasian commissioned one of the most famous—if not the most famous—ancient Roman monuments, famous as the site of extravagant public entertainment (the Colosseum).
Hadrian commissioned several structures that had round and oval shapes (like many of the spaces in his Villa at Tivoli).
The free-standing Arch of Constantine was commissioned by the emperor who decriminalized Christianity, Constantine.

items: ![Triumphal arch, 312–315 C.E., and older spolia, marble and porphyry, Rome](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/aa5fa19a6aa781cd3ccd607da4ece524.jpeg)
![Maritime Theatre, imperial villa, Tivoli, villa begun in 117 C.E.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/121432a6782fb152b554f0c8007173f3.jpeg)
